policy_statement: Miami-Dade County will convert waste to energy through expanded cogeneration, garbage-to-energy conversion, and improved recycling by 2030.
mechanism_description: Converting non-recycled waste into usable energy reduces landfill disposal and associated methane emissions while recovering energy that can offset conventional fossil sources.
typology_evidence_quote: 50% of non-recycled garbage converted to energy by 2030
primary_causal_pathway: Waste disposal practice change → ↓ landfill methane generation + ↑ energy recovery displacing fossil energy → ↓ net GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail: (1) The policy changes the waste management system by diverting non-recycled residual waste away from landfill and into waste-to-energy facilities such as incineration with energy recovery, cogeneration, or similar conversion technologies. (2) The mediating physical processes are, first, prevention of anaerobic decomposition of landfilled organic waste that would otherwise produce methane, and second, generation of electricity and/or heat from waste that offsets some conventional fossil fuel combustion. (3) The climate-relevant outcome is lower net greenhouse gas emissions, primarily from avoided landfill methane and secondarily from fossil energy displacement.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect of policies using this mechanism is reducing net greenhouse gas emissions by diverting waste from landfills to avoid methane generation, while also recovering energy that can displace fossil fuel use.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1. Dominant causal pathway: Residual waste is diverted from landfill to energy conversion, which primarily reduces methane-producing landfill disposal and secondarily produces usable energy that can substitute for fossil generation; the dominant climate effect is lower net GHG emissions.  
2. Why this primary over alternatives: This is Mitigation rather than Resource Efficiency because the principal climate outcome is not simply delivering the same service with fewer material or energy inputs, but directly reducing emissions from waste decomposition and fossil energy use. It is not Adaptation because it does not primarily reduce exposure or vulnerability to climate hazards. It is not Nature-Based Solutions because the mechanism is technological waste processing rather than ecosystem enhancement.  
3. Edge cases applied: Some waste-to-energy systems can have mixed emissions outcomes depending on feedstock composition, facility efficiency, pollution controls, and counterfactual landfill gas capture or energy mix. However, the canonical mechanism explicitly centers landfill methane avoidance and energy recovery, both of which are mitigation pathways.  
4. Confirming applicability to all instances sharing this mechanism: Any policy that fits this mechanism shares the same core causal logic—divert waste from landfill, avoid methane, recover energy, and reduce net emissions—so Mitigation remains the primary category across instances.  
5. Secondary subtype assignment is deferred to policy-level evidence: Specific distinctions such as whether the policy should later be tagged more narrowly under waste methane abatement, energy recovery, circular economy, or incineration-related subtypes depend on the exact technology, feedstock, and local baseline and should be assigned at the policy-evidence stage, not at the mechanism-classification stage.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Waste-to-energy can be contested as a climate strategy because combustion emissions, biogenic versus fossil waste fractions, plant efficiency, and the presence of landfill gas capture can materially affect net benefits. In some contexts, aggressive recycling/composting may outperform combustion. Nonetheless, for this canonical mechanism as defined, the dominant intended pathway is still mitigation via landfill methane avoidance plus fossil energy displacement.
key_indicators: "by 2030"; "48 GWh/year of electricity from cogeneration"; "50% of non-recycled garbage converted to energy"; "10% increase in recycling rates"; "CONVERT WASTE TO ENERGY"
co_benefits: Reduced landfill use, possible local energy resilience benefits, and general waste-system efficiency improvements.
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as part of a broader planning strategy because it appears under an "APPROACH" heading, but the presence of explicit quantitative end-state goals without named implementation tools makes target-setting the closest fit. Waste-to-energy can have non-climate tradeoffs such as air pollution concerns, but those do not affect the instrument classification here.
